# /before-after-project-showcase-post

Turns a finished job's before/after photos and the story of the work into a social or portfolio post that highlights the specific problem solved and skill applied - built to attract the next customer with a similar problem, not just show off a nice photo.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

A job just wrapped and looks great in photos, and you want to post it to Instagram/Facebook/your site portfolio in a way that does real marketing work - attracting someone with the *same underlying problem* - instead of a generic "check out this beautiful kitchen" caption.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the angle (e.g. "what specifically prompted the customer to call you - a failure, a renovation, a sale prep?").
- What the before photos show and what problem or complaint originally brought the customer in
- What was actually done (materials, techniques, anything nonstandard or a judgment call the crew made)
- Any constraint that made the job harder than it looks (tight timeline, matching existing finishes, working around an occupied home, an unexpected issue found mid-job)
- Customer permission status for using their project/name/address publicly
- Target platform (Instagram, Facebook, Google Business post, website portfolio) since format/length differ

## Workflow

1. Open with the *problem*, not the reveal - one sentence naming what was wrong or what the customer wanted, so someone scrolling with the same problem recognizes themselves immediately.
2. Name the specific technique, material, or decision that solved it - "we re-pitched the drainage away from the foundation before repouring" does more selling work than "beautiful new patio," because it signals real expertise to someone who has this exact problem.
3. Mention the hidden difficulty if there was one (found rot mid-demo, matched a 20-year-old brick pattern, finished around a family living in the home) - this is what differentiates you from a competitor posting a similar-looking photo.
4. Write the caption to end on the outcome in the customer's terms (no more standing water, matches the rest of the house, added functional space) rather than purely aesthetic praise.
5. Add a soft, specific call-to-action tied to the problem just shown ("dealing with drainage pooling near your foundation? send us a photo") instead of a generic "DM us for a quote."
6. Confirm photo order (true before → true after, same angle if possible) and check that customer permission is documented before the post format is finalized.

## Output format

**Before/After Showcase - [Job Type] - [Platform]**
- Photo Order Note (which shots, same-angle check, permission status)
- Caption (platform-length-appropriate, structured: problem → what was done → hidden difficulty if any → outcome → specific CTA)
- Alt Hashtag/Keyword Set (if platform-relevant, tied to the specific problem/trade, not generic)
- Permission Status Flag (confirmed / needs to ask, stated explicitly)

## Do
- Lead with the problem the customer had, not the finished-product aesthetics - it's what makes a stranger with the same problem stop scrolling.
- Name real technique/material specifics; vague competence claims don't differentiate you from every other contractor's after-photo.
- Confirm customer permission before finalizing anything that includes their name, address, or recognizable property.

## Don't
- Don't post before securing customer permission, especially if the address or face is identifiable.
- Don't lead with generic praise adjectives ("gorgeous," "stunning") before establishing the real problem solved.
- Don't skip the hidden-difficulty detail if one exists - it's often the single most persuasive line in the whole post.

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Connector access verified before claiming results (if applicable)
- [ ] Approval gate present for any risky/irreversible action (if applicable)
- [ ] Customer permission status is explicitly flagged before the post is treated as ready to publish
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /before-after-project-showcase-post or describe the task from the skill description.

# /job-estimate-from-photos-brief

Converts jobsite photos plus your on-site notes into a structured estimate brief with scope, materials, labor hours, and a price range you can hand off to your estimating software or use to write the customer-facing quote.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

You just walked a job site (a bathroom remod, a fence replacement, a deck repair, a drainage fix) and snapped photos on your phone. You need to turn "I looked at it and it's rough" into a real scope of work before you sit down to write a number, without forgetting a line item you noticed but didn't write down.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the price band materially (e.g. "is this a permit-required job in your municipality?").
- The photos or a description of what's visible in each (damage, materials currently in place, access constraints, square footage if known)
- Trade type and specific task requested by the customer
- Any customer-stated constraints (budget ceiling, must-finish-by date, "match existing" requirements)
- Your local labor rate and any known material cost baseline (if not given, flag estimate as rough-order-of-magnitude only)
- Site access notes (stairs, tight crawlspace, second floor, no parking, HOA restrictions)

## Workflow

1. Inventory what's visible in each photo as a discrete scope item - do not summarize the whole job in one line; separate "remove existing tile," "repair subfloor rot at NE corner," "install cement board" into distinct rows even if they'll later be bundled on the quote.
2. Flag anything that changes scope but isn't confirmed yet - hidden water damage suspected behind a fixture, unknown wall composition, possible asbestos-era material if the home predates 1980 - and mark these as "verify on demo" contingency items, not firm line items.
3. Cross-check the photo-derived scope against the customer's stated request; call out anything the customer asked for that you don't see evidence of a fix needed for, and anything you see that they didn't mention (both are conversations to have before quoting, not surprises after).
4. Break the job into labor blocks (demo, rough-in, finish, cleanup) and estimate hours per block based on visible scope and access difficulty noted in the photos.
5. List materials by rough quantity (not exact SKU-level takeoff) tied to each labor block, so the estimator can price against current supplier costs.
6. Produce a low-high price band, not a single number, and state explicitly which unknowns would move the number up (contingency items materializing) versus down (customer supplies materials, simpler access than assumed).
7. Note any item requiring a permit, inspection, or subtrade (electrician, plumber) so it doesn't get missed at quoting time.

## Output format

**Job Estimate Brief - [Customer/Address] - [Date]**
- Scope Summary (2-3 sentences, plain language, what the customer is buying)
- Line Items table: `Item | Labor Block | Est. Hours | Material Notes | Confidence (firm/contingent)`
- Contingency Watch List (bullet list of "verify on demo" items and how they'd change price)
- Access & Site Notes (anything affecting labor time)
- Permits/Subtrades Needed
- Price Band: $X,XXX - $X,XXX with one sentence on what pushes toward each end
- Open Questions for Customer (max 3, only things that materially change scope)

## Do
- Separate firm scope from contingent scope every time - this is the single most common source of underbidding in trades work.
- Note access/labor-difficulty factors explicitly since they're invisible in a materials list but real in hours.
- Keep the price band honest - a fake-precise single number from photos alone is worse than a clearly-labeled range.

## Don't
- Don't invent exact measurements you can't see in a photo - use "approx." and flag for field verification.
- Don't quietly drop a customer-requested item just because it's not visible in the photos; surface it as a question instead.
- Don't price hidden-damage contingencies as if they were confirmed scope.

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Connector access verified before claiming results (if applicable)
- [ ] Approval gate present for any risky/irreversible action (if applicable)
- [ ] Every contingent/unverified item is clearly separated from firm scope so the price band can't be mistaken for a fixed quote
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /job-estimate-from-photos-brief or describe the task from the skill description.

---
name: local-SEO-listing-audit
description: >
  Audits a trades business's Google Business Profile and directory listings for the specific gaps that hurt local map-pack ranking, and produces a prioritized fix list. Triggers: "audit my Google Business Profile", "why aren't we showing up in local search", /local-SEO-listing-audit.
metadata:
  category: trades-contractor-quoting
  type: connector
  cadence: monthly
  risk: low

allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /local-SEO-listing-audit

Pulls your live Google Business Profile data (or works from a screenshot/export if the connector isn't available) and produces a prioritized list of local-SEO fixes specific to what's actually missing or weak on your listing.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

You're not showing up in the local "map pack" for searches like "[your trade] near me" or "[your trade] in [city]," a competitor with fewer years in business ranks above you, or it's just been a while since anyone checked whether the listing is complete and accurate.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the audit (e.g. "which service area cities matter most for you?").
- Business name, trade category, and primary service area (city/region, or multiple if you serve a metro)
- The exact search terms you want to rank for (e.g., "emergency plumber [city]" vs. "bathroom remodel [city]")
- Whether you can grant connector access to Google Business Profile, or will paste a screenshot/export of the current listing instead
- Any known competitor names ranking above you, if you've noticed them
- How often the listing gets updated currently (photos, posts, Q&A) - if unknown, treat as "rarely"

## Connector access (required)

This skill cannot run from text alone - it must call live integrations.
1. Check your AI tool's connector/integration settings (for example: Grok's `/mcps` command, Cursor's MCP settings, Claude's Integrations panel, or your tool's equivalent) and confirm Google Business Profile access is enabled and authenticated. The exact steps vary by tool - if you're not sure how, just ask your AI assistant directly.
2. Use `search_tool` to discover the available tools for the Google Business Profile connector in your current session.
3. Call tools with `use_tool` using the exact tool name your session exposes.
4. Never claim to have accessed Google Business Profile without a successful tool call - if the connector isn't available, stop and tell the user which integration to enable, and offer the manual fallback below (audit from a pasted screenshot or export instead).

### Required integrations
- **@google-business-profile** - Google Business Profile
  - Example tools you might see: get_business_profile, list_reviews, get_insights, update_business_info

## Workflow

1. Pull the current listing data: category selection (primary + secondary), business description, service area list, hours, attributes, photo count/recency, Q&A section, and review count/average/response rate.
2. Check category accuracy first - a mismatched or too-broad primary category (e.g., "Contractor" instead of "Plumber") is the single most common cause of a trades business missing the map pack for trade-specific searches.
3. Compare the service area list against the actual target search terms - if you want to rank for "[city B]" but the profile only lists "[city A]" as a service area, that's a direct, fixable gap.
4. Check review velocity and response rate, not just star average - a stale review history (few reviews in the last 90 days) signals inactivity to the ranking algorithm even with a high average; check whether every review (especially negative ones) has an owner response.
5. Check photo recency and volume - profiles with recent, categorized photos (before/after, team, completed jobs) tend to outrank thin profiles with old or stock-like photos; note the last photo upload date.
6. Check for Google Posts activity (or its current equivalent) - an empty or stale posts feed is a missed, easy, and free ranking signal.
7. Rank every finding by fix effort vs. expected ranking impact, and flag anything that requires Google verification/support (like a suspended or duplicate listing) as urgent and separate from routine cleanup.

## Output format

**Local SEO Listing Audit - [Business Name] - [Date]**
- Snapshot: category, service areas listed, review count/average, last photo date, last post date
- Findings table: `Issue | Why It Hurts Ranking | Fix Effort (low/med/high) | Priority (urgent/soon/ongoing)`
- Category & Service Area Gaps (specific mismatches vs. target search terms)
- Review Health (velocity, response rate, unanswered negative reviews flagged by name/date)
- Content Freshness Gaps (photos, posts, Q&A)
- This Week's Top 3 Actions (the highest-impact, lowest-effort fixes, made concrete enough to do today)

## Do
- Pull live data through the connector whenever possible rather than guessing from memory of "how GBP usually looks."
- Prioritize category and service-area accuracy above cosmetic fixes - they have outsized ranking impact for trades searches.
- Call out unanswered negative reviews by name/date since they're both a ranking and a reputation issue.

## Don't
- Don't recommend keyword-stuffing the business name or description - this violates Google's guidelines and risks suspension.
- Don't treat this as one-and-done; note it's designed to run monthly since listings decay.
- Don't claim connector data was pulled if the call failed - fall back to the pasted-screenshot method and say so explicitly.

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Connector access verified before claiming results (if applicable)
- [ ] Approval gate present for any risky/irreversible action (if applicable)
- [ ] Category and service-area accuracy were checked first, before cosmetic photo/post suggestions
```

3. Compare the service area list against the actual target search terms - if you want to rank for "[city B]" but the profile only lists "[city A]" as a service area, that's a direct, fixable gap.
4. Check review velocity and response rate, not just star average - a stale review history (few reviews in the last 90 days) signals inactivity to the ranking algorithm even with a high average; check whether every review (especially negative ones) has an owner response.

6. Check for Google Posts activity (or its current equivalent) - an empty or stale posts feed is a missed, easy, and free ranking signal.

- Review Health (velocity, response rate, unanswered negative reviews flagged by name/date)
- Content Freshness Gaps (photos, posts, Q&A)

- Pull live data through the connector whenever possible rather than guessing from memory of "how GBP usually looks."
- Prioritize category and service-area accuracy above cosmetic fixes - they have outsized ranking impact for trades searches.

- Don't recommend keyword-stuffing the business name or description - this violates Google's guidelines and risks suspension.
- Don't treat this as one-and-done; note it's designed to run monthly since listings decay.

# /material-cost-tracker-narrative

Converts a job's raw material receipts and the original estimate into a plain-language narrative showing where costs tracked, where they overran, and what that means for margin - so you catch drift mid-job instead of at the final invoice.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

A job is underway or just wrapped, you've got a stack of supplier receipts and change-order paperwork, and you need to understand fast whether material costs are tracking to the original estimate or quietly eating the margin - especially useful mid-job, while there's still time to course-correct or have the price conversation with the customer.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the read (e.g. "was this price increase already covered by a signed change order?").
- The original estimate's material budget, broken down by category if available (lumber, fixtures, electrical, finish materials)
- The receipts/invoices so far (vendor, item, cost, date) - pasted, listed, or summarized
- Whether any change orders were signed that should have adjusted the material budget
- The job's current completion percentage (roughly) so cost-to-date can be read in context, not in isolation
- Known supplier price volatility for this job's materials, if relevant (e.g., lumber prices moved since the quote was written)

## Workflow

1. Match actual spend against the original budget category by category, not just as one lump total - a job can be "on budget overall" while one category (say, tile) is 40% over and another is under, masking a real problem.
2. Separate cost overruns that were already covered by a signed change order (not a margin problem, just a pass-through) from overruns that weren't (a real margin hit or an unbilled change order waiting to happen).
3. Flag any receipt that doesn't obviously map to this job's scope - a common source of margin leakage is materials bought for this job that were actually used elsewhere, or vice versa.
4. Calculate a projected final material cost based on completion percentage and current burn rate, not just cost-to-date, so the narrative answers "where will this land" not just "where are we now."
5. For any category running hot, name the likely cause in plain terms (price increase since quoting, scope creep not yet change-ordered, waste/rework, wrong initial takeoff) - this determines what to do about it.
6. If the projection shows the job will land under negative or thin margin, say so directly and flag it as needing an owner decision (eat it, invoice a change order, adjust scope) rather than burying it in a neutral summary.

## Output format

**Material Cost Tracker - [Job/Address] - [Date] - [Est. % Complete]**
- Budget vs. Actual by Category table: `Category | Budgeted | Spent to Date | Projected Final | Variance | Covered by Change Order? (Y/N)`
- Uncategorized/Unclear Receipts (anything that didn't cleanly map to a category)
- Root Cause Notes (plain-language reason for each category running hot or cold)
- Margin Projection: on track / at risk / underwater, stated directly
- Recommended Action (invoice a change order, absorb it, flag to customer, or none needed)

## Do
- Break the analysis down by category - a lump-sum "on budget" figure hides real problems.
- Distinguish change-order-covered overruns from unbilled ones; they require completely different responses.
- State the margin risk plainly rather than softening it into a neutral-sounding summary.

## Don't
- Don't wait until job completion to run this - the whole value is catching drift while there's still time to act.
- Don't quietly absorb an unbilled scope-creep overrun into the narrative as if it were expected; flag it as a decision point.
- Don't treat a projected number with the same certainty as an actual - label projections clearly as estimates.

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Connector access verified before claiming results (if applicable)
- [ ] Approval gate present for any risky/irreversible action (if applicable)
- [ ] Change-order-covered variance is clearly separated from unbilled variance in every category, not blended into one variance number
```

# /review-request-sequence

Produces a timed, multi-step review request sequence (text/email) for a specific completed job, including a private-feedback gate that catches dissatisfied customers before they leave a public negative review.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

You just finished a job that went well (or well enough) and want to systematically ask for a Google/Yelp/Facebook review without a) annoying a happy customer with review-nagging, or b) accidentally pushing an unhappy customer straight to a public review before you get a chance to fix the problem.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the sequence (e.g. "did the customer mention any concerns during or right after the job?").
- Job type and how it went from the crew's perspective (any friction, delays, change orders, or complaints during the job)
- Which review platform matters most for this business (Google is usually primary; note if Yelp/Facebook/Angi/industry-specific site matters more)
- Timing constraints - is there a reason to wait (e.g., "let the new deck cure/settle for a week before asking so they're not judging mid-project mess")
- Whether you have a review-gating tool/process already, or need the private-feedback step to be a plain reply-to text
- The customer's name and a specific detail from the job to reference (not just "your project")

## Workflow

1. Decide the right delay before the first ask based on job type - same-day for quick repairs where satisfaction is obvious immediately, but a delay of several days to weeks for larger jobs (remodels, installs) so the customer has lived with the result before being asked to rate it.
2. Build in a private-feedback checkpoint before the public review ask: a simple "on a scale of 1-5, how did everything go?" text where anything below a threshold routes to "tell us more" (private) instead of the public review link, and anything at/above routes straight to the review link.
3. Write the low-score branch as a genuine service-recovery message, not a brush-off - acknowledge, ask what went wrong, and commit to a specific next step (call within X hours), because this is your last chance to fix it before it becomes public.
4. Write the high-score branch to go straight to the review platform link with minimal friction - one line of context plus the direct link, no extra steps.
5. Add exactly one follow-up nudge (not more) for customers who didn't respond to the first ask, spaced several days out, worded differently so it doesn't read as a repeat.
6. Keep total touches to 2-3 max across the whole sequence - more than that reads as nagging and can itself trigger a negative review about being "spammed for reviews."

## Output format

**Review Request Sequence - [Job Type] - [Customer Name]**
- Timing: first ask sent [X days/hours] after job completion, rationale in one line
- Step 1 - Private Feedback Gate (exact message text, with the branching threshold stated)
- Step 2a - Low Score Branch (service recovery message + internal action item for the owner)
- Step 2b - High Score Branch (review ask message with platform link placeholder)
- Step 3 - Single Follow-Up Nudge (only if no response, timing + message)

## Do
- Always gate the public review ask behind a private satisfaction check first - this protects your public rating and gives you a chance to fix problems.
- Reference the specific job in every message; generic "thanks for choosing us" reads as automated even when it is.
- Cap the sequence at 2-3 touches total.

## Don't
- Don't send a direct public review link to every customer without a gating step - one bad job goes public and sits there for years.
- Don't send more than one follow-up nudge; diminishing returns turn into complaints about being spammed.
- Don't make the low-score branch feel like damage control theater - it needs a real, specific commitment to follow up.

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Connector access verified before claiming results (if applicable)
- [ ] Approval gate present for any risky/irreversible action (if applicable)
- [ ] A private-feedback gate sits before any public review link so unhappy customers never get routed straight to a public platform
```

# /seasonal-promo-plan

Builds a promotion plan timed to the specific equipment failures and homeowner pain points that spike in your trade during the upcoming season, including offer structure, timing, and which past customers to reach first.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

You're heading into a seasonal shift (first cold snap, start of storm season, spring thaw, pre-holiday rush) and want to fill the schedule with the right jobs before the demand spike hits, rather than reacting to emergency calls after equipment already fails.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the plan (e.g. "do you have a customer list you can filter by install date or system age?").
- Trade type and the specific season/weather transition being planned for
- Your capacity constraint for the period (crew size, how many extra jobs you can realistically absorb)
- Past customer data available: install dates, system ages, last service date, past job type - even a rough list helps
- Current slow points in the calendar you're trying to fill vs. rush points you're trying to get ahead of
- Any existing promo history (what's been tried before and how it performed, if known)

## Workflow

1. Identify the specific failure or need pattern this trade sees in this exact season (e.g., landscapers: irrigation blowouts before first freeze; HVAC: furnace ignitor failures in first cold snap; roofers: post-storm inspection surges; remodelers: "done before the holidays" deadline pressure) - the offer should map to a real, predictable pain point, not a generic discount.
2. Segment the customer list by who is actually at risk this season, not the whole database - e.g., systems installed 8-12 years ago for a "beat the breakdown" furnace tune-up offer, or customers serviced last spring for an irrigation winterization reminder.
3. Design the offer structure around urgency and capacity, not just price: an early-bird slot discount that rewards booking before the rush (protects your calendar) reads differently than a flat percent-off (just cuts margin).
4. Set a concrete timing window - when the campaign goes out, how many touches, and the cutoff date that creates real urgency (tied to the weather/seasonal pattern, not an arbitrary date).
5. Draft the actual outreach copy for the top 1-2 channels you'd use (text, email, postcard, social) in the customer's language, referencing the specific seasonal risk, not "seasonal savings."
6. Build in a simple capacity guardrail - a cap on how many promo bookings you'll take before the price/urgency reverts, so a successful promo doesn't overbook the crew.

## Output format

**Seasonal Promo Plan - [Trade] - [Season/Date Range]**
- Seasonal Pain Point This Targets (1-2 sentences, specific failure/need pattern)
- Target Customer Segment (who gets this offer and why, with the filter logic)
- Offer Structure (what's discounted/bundled/prioritized, and the capacity guardrail)
- Timing Window (launch date, touch schedule, urgency cutoff)
- Outreach Copy (ready-to-send for top 1-2 channels)
- Success Measure (how you'll know if it worked - bookings, revenue, or calendar fill rate)

## Do
- Tie every promo to a real seasonal mechanism (weather, equipment age, calendar deadline) - that's what makes it convert better than a generic discount.
- Build in a capacity cap so the promo can't overbook the crew.
- Prioritize past-customer segments with real risk signals over blasting the whole list.

## Don't
- Don't default to "10% off everything" - undifferentiated discounts erode margin without creating urgency.
- Don't ignore crew capacity when designing the offer; a promo that overbooks the calendar creates a service-quality problem later.
- Don't target customers with no seasonal risk signal just to pad the list size.

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Connector access verified before claiming results (if applicable)
- [ ] Approval gate present for any risky/irreversible action (if applicable)
- [ ] The offer is anchored to a real, named seasonal failure/need pattern for this specific trade, not a generic discount
```

# /service-call-followup-script

Produces a short, specific follow-up script (call talking points plus a text/email version) for checking in on a completed repair or service visit, built around exactly what was done and what could still go wrong.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

A tech just closed out a work order - a water heater swap, a breaker panel repair, a sprinkler head fix - and you want to check in with the customer within 24-48 hours to catch problems early, reinforce trust, and open the door to the next job, without sounding like a generic "how did we do" survey.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the script (e.g. "was this a repair or a full replacement?").
- What was actually done on the visit (repair vs. replace, parts used, system type)
- The specific failure mode that brought them in (so you can ask about *that* symptom, not a generic "everything okay?")
- Anything the tech flagged as "watch this" on the way out (a borderline part, an aging component nearby, a temporary fix pending a part order)
- Whether there's a warranty or maintenance plan to mention
- Preferred contact method and the tech's name (so the call sounds like it's from the person who was there, not a call center)

## Workflow

1. Open the script by naming the specific job and tech, not a generic "we serviced your home" - customers trust specificity.
2. Build the check-in question around the original symptom (e.g., "is the water heating back up to temperature within the first 10 minutes?") instead of a vague satisfaction question.
3. Include one targeted question tied to anything the tech flagged as borderline, framed as proactive care, not a sales pitch ("Mike mentioned the pressure valve looked older than the rest of the unit - has it been holding steady?").
4. Add a clear "if this happens, call us back the same day, no charge for the callback" line for anything with a real chance of recurring within the warranty window.
5. Only after the check-in, add one soft, non-pushy next-step mention (seasonal maintenance, a related system inspection) - never lead with upsell.
6. Write two versions: a phone-call talking-points version (bullet points, not a verbatim script, so it sounds natural) and a text/email version (short, sends as-is).

## Output format

**Follow-Up: [Customer Name] - [Job Type] - [Date of Service]**
- Call Talking Points (bullets, in order, includes the specific symptom check and the borderline-part check)
- Text/Email Version (ready to send, under 60 words)
- Callback Trigger Line (what to tell them to watch for and how fast to respond if it happens)
- Optional Next-Step Mention (one line, only if genuinely relevant)

## Do
- Reference the exact symptom and part, not the job category - "is the water heating up fast enough" beats "how's everything working."
- Make the no-charge callback window explicit if one exists; it reduces angry surprise calls and builds trust.
- Keep the text version short enough to read in one glance on a phone.

## Don't
- Don't turn this into a review-request message - that's a different job (see review-request-sequence); keep this focused on the technical check-in.
- Don't lead with or bury the check-in under a sales pitch.
- Don't use generic "How did we do?" phrasing when you have specific symptom data available.

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Connector access verified before claiming results (if applicable)
- [ ] Approval gate present for any risky/irreversible action (if applicable)
- [ ] The check-in question is tied to the actual symptom/part from this job, not a generic satisfaction question
```

# /subcontractor-scope-brief

Produces a specific, boundary-drawing scope-of-work brief for a subcontractor on one portion of a larger job, so responsibility, sequencing, and cleanup expectations are explicit before work starts.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

You're bringing in a subcontractor (electrician, drywaller, tile setter, excavator) for one piece of a larger job and want to avoid the classic dispute pattern - "that wasn't in my scope" - by writing down exactly where their responsibility starts and stops before they show up.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the boundary lines (e.g. "who's responsible for disposal of the debris this sub generates - you or them?").
- The overall job and where this sub's piece fits in the sequence (what happens immediately before and after their work)
- The specific trade and task (rough-in electrical, drywall hang-and-finish, tile install, grading)
- What's explicitly included vs. explicitly excluded (materials supplied by whom, permit responsibility, cleanup/debris responsibility)
- The general contractor's quality standard or reference point (matching existing work, code minimum, a specific finish level)
- Schedule constraints - start date, how many days they have, what blocks the next trade from starting

## Workflow

1. State the physical/scope boundary in concrete terms tied to the job, not trade generalities - "electrical rough-in for the two new bathroom circuits and the exterior GFCI, from panel to box, not including fixture install" beats "do the electrical."
2. List explicit exclusions as their own section - the things a reasonable sub might assume are included but aren't (patching drywall after rough-in, hauling their own debris, coordinating inspection scheduling) - because exclusions are where disputes actually happen, not inclusions.
3. Specify material responsibility line by line for anything ambiguous (who supplies fixtures, fasteners, disposal bins) rather than a blanket "GC supplies materials."
4. Set the quality/finish standard explicitly with a concrete reference (matches the sample board, meets current code, matches the adjacent existing wall) instead of "professional workmanship," which is unenforceable in a dispute.
5. Define the sequencing dependency - what must be true before this sub can start, and what their completion unblocks for the next trade - so schedule slippage has a clear point of origin.
6. State the walkthrough/sign-off condition - what "done" looks like and who inspects before payment triggers, so there's no ambiguity about when the scope is considered fulfilled.
7. Note any change-order trigger explicitly: what would count as work beyond this scope, and the process for pricing and approving it before the sub does it.

## Output format

**Subcontractor Scope Brief - [Sub Trade] - [Job/Address] - [Date]**
- Job Context (one paragraph: where this sub's work fits in the overall sequence)
- Included Scope (specific, itemized)
- Explicit Exclusions (itemized - the "not in scope" list)
- Materials Responsibility (who supplies what)
- Quality/Finish Standard (concrete reference point)
- Schedule: Start Trigger / Duration / Completion Unblocks
- Sign-Off Condition (what "done" means, who inspects)
- Change Order Process (one line: how added scope gets priced and approved)

## Do
- Write exclusions as explicitly as inclusions - that's where most disputes actually live.
- Tie the quality standard to something concrete and inspectable, never to "professional standards" alone.
- State the change-order trigger up front so scope creep gets priced before it happens, not argued about after.

## Don't
- Don't leave debris/cleanup responsibility implied - state it directly, it's a top source of GC/sub friction.
- Don't use vague finish-quality language that can't be checked at walkthrough.
- Don't skip the sequencing section - a sub who doesn't know what unblocks next will deprioritize your job when their schedule gets tight.

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Connector access verified before claiming results (if applicable)
- [ ] Approval gate present for any risky/irreversible action (if applicable)
- [ ] Exclusions and materials responsibility are each spelled out as their own section, not folded into general scope language
```

# /warranty-claim-response

Drafts a warranty-claim response that clearly determines whether the reported issue is covered, explains why in plain terms, and lays out next steps - protecting both the customer relationship and the business from unfunded scope creep.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

A past customer contacts you saying something you installed or repaired has failed, and you need to determine - and communicate - whether this is a covered warranty issue, a separate new problem, misuse, or normal wear, before a truck rolls or a promise gets made.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the coverage determination (e.g. "is the reported issue the same failure mode as the original repair, or something new?").
- The original job: what was done, when, and the warranty terms that applied (duration, what's covered - parts, labor, both)
- The customer's description of the current problem and when it started
- Whether the failure is the same component/system originally worked on, or something adjacent/unrelated
- Any evidence of external factors that could void coverage (unrelated work by another contractor since, visible misuse or lack of required maintenance, an act of nature)
- The tone/history of this customer relationship (long-time client vs. first job) - this affects goodwill-gesture decisions, not coverage facts

## Safety

- Propose before acting: show the coverage determination and draft response for the owner's review before it's sent to the customer.
- Never take an irreversible or external-facing action (sending the response, committing to free work, refunding) without the user explicitly saying to proceed.

## Workflow

1. State the coverage determination first, plainly, before drafting any customer-facing language - covered, not covered, or partially covered/needs inspection to confirm - so the business owner is deciding on facts, not being talked into a stance by the draft's tone.
2. Check the failure against the specific warranty terms (duration elapsed, component covered, labor vs. parts only) rather than a general "did we mess up" judgment call.
3. If not clearly covered, identify the specific reason (time-expired, different component, evidence of misuse/other work, normal wear) so the customer response can explain *why*, not just say no.
4. If coverage is unclear, default the response to "we need to inspect before determining coverage" rather than guessing in either direction - this avoids both over-promising and prematurely denying a legitimate claim.
5. Draft the customer response in a tone that acknowledges their frustration without admitting fault that hasn't been established, and without being defensive.
6. Separate the warranty determination from any goodwill gesture the owner might choose to offer anyway (e.g., "not covered, but we'll do the trip charge only as a courtesy since you've been a repeat customer") - make clear these are two different decisions.
7. Include a clear internal note (not sent to the customer) logging the determination and reasoning, so there's a record if this recurs or escalates.

## Output format

**Warranty Claim Response - [Customer Name] - [Original Job Date]**
- Coverage Determination: Covered / Not Covered / Needs Inspection - with the specific reason
- Warranty Terms Referenced (what the original agreement said, in plain language)
- Customer-Facing Response (ready to send - acknowledges concern, states determination, states next step)
- Optional Goodwill Gesture (if the owner wants to offer one, kept separate from the coverage facts)
- Internal Note (determination + reasoning, for the business's own records)

## Do
- Separate the factual coverage determination from the relationship/goodwill decision - conflating them leads to inconsistent policy over time.
- Default to "needs inspection" when evidence is ambiguous rather than guessing.
- Keep an internal record of the reasoning in case the claim escalates or repeats.

## Don't
- Don't send anything to the customer without the owner's explicit sign-off - this is a high-risk, relationship- and money-affecting message.
- Don't imply fault or admit liability language ("we're sorry we broke it") when the cause isn't established.
- Don't let a long-time-customer goodwill gesture get recorded as if it were a coverage precedent for future claims.

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Connector access verified before claiming results (if applicable)
- [ ] Approval gate present for any risky/irreversible action (if applicable)
- [ ] The coverage determination and any goodwill gesture are presented as two clearly separate decisions, not blended into one
```

- The tone/history of this customer relationship (long-time client vs. first job) - this affects goodwill-gesture decisions, not coverage facts

## Safety

- Propose before acting: show the coverage determination and draft response for the owner's review before it's sent to the customer.
- Never take an irreversible or external-facing action (sending the response, committing to free work, refunding) without the user explicitly saying to proceed.

## Workflow

1. State the coverage determination first, plainly, before drafting any customer-facing language - covered, not covered, or partially covered/needs inspection to confirm - so the business owner is deciding on facts, not being talked into a stance by the draft's tone.
2. Check the failure against the specific warranty terms (duration elapsed, component covered, labor vs. parts only) rather than a general "did we mess up" judgment call.
3. If not clearly covered, identify the specific reason (time-expired, different component, evidence of misuse/other work, normal wear) so the customer response can explain *why*, not just say no.
4. If coverage is unclear, default the response to "we need to inspect before determining coverage" rather than guessing in either direction - this avoids both over-promising and prematurely denying a legitimate claim.
5. Draft the customer response in a tone that acknowledges their frustration without admitting fault that hasn't been established, and without being defensive.
6. Separate the warranty determination from any goodwill gesture the owner might choose to offer anyway (e.g., "not covered, but we'll do the trip charge only as a courtesy since you've been a repeat customer") - make clear these are two different decisions.
7. Include a clear internal note (not sent to the customer) logging the determination and reasoning, so there's a record if this recurs or escalates.

## Output format

**Warranty Claim Response - [Customer Name] - [Original Job Date]**
- Coverage Determination: Covered / Not Covered / Needs Inspection - with the specific reason
- Warranty Terms Referenced (what the original agreement said, in plain language)
- Customer-Facing Response (ready to send - acknowledges concern, states determination, states next step)
- Optional Goodwill Gesture (if the owner wants to offer one, kept separate from the coverage facts)
- Internal Note (determination + reasoning, for the business's own records)

## Do
- Separate the factual coverage determination from the relationship/goodwill decision - conflating them leads to inconsistent policy over time.
- Default to "needs inspection" when evidence is ambiguous rather than guessing.
- Keep an internal record of the reasoning in case the claim escalates or repeats.

## Don't
- Don't send anything to the customer without the owner's explicit sign-off - this is a high-risk, relationship- and money-affecting message.
- Don't imply fault or admit liability language ("we're sorry we broke it") when the cause isn't established.
- Don't let a long-time-customer goodwill gesture get recorded as if it were a coverage precedent for future claims.

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Connector access verified before claiming results (if applicable)
- [ ] Approval gate present for any risky/irreversible action (if applicable)
- [ ] The coverage determination and any goodwill gesture are presented as two clearly separate decisions, not blended into one
